title: Adding, Removing, and Modifying List Elements
description: Dynamically change lists by adding, deleting, or updating components.

slides.title: Adding Elements ➕
slides.content:
You can add new components to a list using `$`, `[[`, or `[` assignment.

```r
l <- list(a = 1, b = 2)

# Add using $
l$c <- 3

# Add using [[
l[["d"]] <- 4

# Add using [ (must assign a list)
l["e"] <- list(5)
```

If the component name already exists, it will be overwritten.

slides.title: Adding Elements
slides.codeSnippet:
l <- list(a = 1:3, b = "hello")
l$c <- pi
l[["d"]] <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2)
l["e"] <- list(c(TRUE, FALSE))
str(l)

quizData.question: What is the correct way to add a new component with value 100 using single brackets?
quizData.explanation: When using single bracket, the right-hand side must be a list of the same length, so `list(100)`.

slides.title: Removing Elements ✂️
slides.content:
Set a component to `NULL` to remove it.

```r
l$c <- NULL   # removes component c
l[["d"]] <- NULL   # removes d
```

You can also assign `NULL` to a subset using `[`, but that removes the element and shifts others.

slides.title: Removing Elements
slides.codeSnippet:
l <- list(a = 1, b = 2, c = 3)
print(names(l))
l$b <- NULL
print(names(l))

# Remove by index using single bracket
l[1] <- NULL
print(names(l))

slides.title: Modifying Elements
slides.content:
You can modify a component by assigning a new value using any of the subsetting methods.

```r
l$a <- c(10,20)   # replace a with a vector
l[[2]] <- "new"   # replace second component
```

slides.title: Modifying
slides.codeSnippet:
l <- list(x = 1:3, y = "old")
l$x <- l$x * 10
l$y <- "new"
print(l)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Removing an element with `NULL` using `[` is different from `$`. `l[2] <- NULL` removes the element and the list length decreases. `l[[2]] <- NULL` does the same. Actually, both remove. But `l$b <- NULL` also removes. So all methods remove when assigned NULL.
